transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
I have lower abdominal pain.
When did the lower abdominal pain start?
2 days ago.
Can you describe the type of pain and if it moves anywhere?
It's sharp and stabbing, and it doesn't move anywhere.
Any fever, chills, or nausea since the pain started?
Yes. All.
Can you tell me if you have noticed any vaginal bleeding, discharge, or urinary symptoms like burning or increased frequency?
No.
Have you had any recent sexual activity or any history of sexually transmitted infections?
No.
Have you experienced any vomiting or changes in bowel movements such as diarrhea or constipation?
No.
Any history of abdominal surgeries, chronic illnesses, or recent travel?
No.
Is there any pain or tenderness when you press on your lower abdomen?
Yes.
Are you currently pregnant or is there any chance you could be pregnant?
No.
Have you noticed any changes in your menstrual cycle recently, such as missed periods or abnormal bleeding?
I've had a little bit more bleeding than usual.
How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
Penicillin.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

Based on what you’ve described, your symptoms suggest a possible pelvic infection or other gynecologic issue that needs careful attention. Because I can’t examine you or run tests over the phone, a licensed physician will review your information and may prescribe medication if appropriate. In the meantime, if your abdominal pain worsens, your fever stays high or gets worse, or if you develop new symptoms like vomiting or heavy bleeding, please seek urgent in-person care right away. If you need an exam or testing, going to an urgent care or emergency department is the best option. Take acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil) if you can safely use them for pain or fever. A doctor will review your case and decide on the next steps, including any needed tests or treatment.
